enrichment.short_description: Homebase is an all-in-one scheduling, time tracking and payroll platform built for hourly teams at restaurants, retail and small businesses.
enrichment.seo_title: Homebase Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Homebase offers free and paid plans for employee scheduling, time clocks, team messaging and payroll built for hourly workers at small businesses.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Homebase is an all-in-one workforce management platform purpose-built for hourly and shift-based small businesses, including restaurants, retail stores, salons, healthcare practices and other local businesses that need to schedule, communicate with and pay an hourly workforce. The company was founded in 2014 by John Waldmann and Rushi Patel and is headquartered in San Francisco, California. Homebase launched its core scheduling and time-tracking product in 2015 and has since expanded into a full suite covering employee scheduling, time clocks (web, mobile and tablet-based punch clocks), team messaging, hiring and onboarding, payroll, HR compliance document storage, and labor cost forecasting. The platform integrates with point-of-sale systems such as Square, Clover, Toast and Lightspeed, syncing sales data to help managers build labor-cost-aware schedules and forecast staffing needs. Homebase has raised roughly 189 million dollars in venture funding across five rounds: an 8 million dollar seed round, a 6 million dollar Series A in 2016 led by Khosla Ventures, a 20 million dollar Series B in 2018 led by Bain Capital Ventures, a 71 million dollar Series C in 2021 led by GGV Capital with participation from actor Matthew McConaughey, and a 60 million dollar Series D in April 2024 led by L Catterton and Emerson Collective. As of 2025 the company reported more than 3.5 million small-business employees using Homebase to log over 1.2 billion work hours, and the company employs roughly 1,900 to 1,900-plus people globally. Architecturally, Homebase is a cloud-based SaaS delivered through a responsive web dashboard plus native iOS and Android apps for both managers and employees, with a free mobile time clock app that supports GPS location tracking and photo-verified clock-ins to prevent buddy punching. Pricing follows a freemium model with four core tiers: Basic is free forever for up to 10 employees at one location with basic scheduling, time tracking and POS integration; Essentials costs 30 dollars per month, or 24 dollars per month billed annually, and unlocks unlimited employees, advanced scheduling, advanced time tracking and team communication; Plus costs 70 dollars per month, or 56 dollars annually, and adds AI-powered auto-scheduling, PTO controls, and department and permission management; and All-in-One costs 120 dollars per month, or 96 dollars annually, and adds employee onboarding, labor cost management and HR and compliance tools. Homebase also sells add-ons a la carte, including full-service payroll at 39 dollars per month base plus 6 dollars per employee paid, a tip manager at 25 dollars per month per location, background checks at 30 dollars each, a task manager at 13 dollars per month per location, and job-post boosts and a hiring assistant for recruiting. New users get a 14-day free trial of the All-in-One plan before defaulting to the free Basic tier if they do not subscribe. Homebase's target audience is small and mid-sized businesses in food service, retail, healthcare, home and repair services, and other industries that rely on hourly, deskless labor and need to reduce time spent on manual scheduling and paper timesheets while staying compliant with local scheduling and labor laws. Its most direct competitors are When I Work, Deputy, 7shifts, which focuses specifically on restaurants, Sling, and workforce modules bundled into point-of-sale systems like Toast Payroll and Square Team Management. Homebase differentiates through the combination of a genuinely usable free tier, deep POS integrations, and a broader bundled feature set spanning scheduling, payroll and hiring in one product rather than requiring several separate point solutions.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Homebase was built specifically for businesses that run on hourly labor rather than salaried, desk-based teams. Founded in 2014 by John Waldmann and Rushi Patel in San Francisco, the company started as a scheduling and time-tracking tool and has since grown into a full workforce operating system covering scheduling, time clocks, team messaging, hiring, onboarding, payroll and HR compliance.

The platform is designed around the realities of restaurants, retail stores, salons and similar small businesses: unpredictable shift swaps, tip pooling, overtime rules that vary by state or city, and a workforce that mostly clocks in on a shared tablet or phone rather than a desktop computer. Homebase syncs with popular point-of-sale systems to bring sales data directly into scheduling, so managers can staff up or down based on projected demand instead of guesswork.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core features include drag-and-drop and AI-assisted schedule building, a shared team calendar with shift-swap and time-off requests, GPS- and photo-verified time clocks that prevent buddy punching, and built-in team messaging so managers can broadcast shift changes instantly. Labor cost forecasting tools show projected wage spend against sales in real time, helping owners avoid overtime surprises.

Beyond scheduling, Homebase offers hiring tools including job postings, applicant tracking and a hiring assistant, plus HR features like digital employee onboarding, document storage, and compliance tracking for break and scheduling laws. An integrated payroll add-on runs payroll directly from tracked hours, eliminating manual data entry between systems.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Homebase uses a freemium structure with a permanently free Basic plan for very small teams, and three paid tiers, Essentials, Plus and All-in-One, that unlock unlimited employees and progressively more advanced scheduling, HR and compliance functionality. Annual billing knocks roughly 20 percent off the monthly price on every paid tier.

Payroll, tip management, background checks, task management and recruiting tools are sold as optional add-ons rather than bundled by default, letting businesses pay only for what they use. A 14-day free trial of the top All-in-One tier lets new customers evaluate the full feature set before deciding which plan fits their team size and complexity.

features.title: AI-assisted scheduling
features.description: Auto-builds shift schedules based on availability, labor budget and sales forecasts, cutting the time spent manually assembling a weekly roster.

features.title: GPS and photo time clocks
features.description: Employees clock in and out from a phone or shared tablet with GPS location checks and photo capture to prevent buddy punching.

features.title: Team messaging
features.description: Built-in chat and shift-change broadcasts keep hourly staff informed without relying on group texts or paper notices.

features.title: POS integrations
features.description: Syncs with Square, Clover, Toast and Lightspeed so schedules and labor costs can be built around real sales data.

features.title: Payroll add-on
features.description: Runs payroll directly from tracked hours and tips, reducing manual re-entry between time tracking and pay runs.

features.title: Hiring and onboarding
features.description: Post jobs, screen applicants, run background checks and digitally onboard new hires from one dashboard.

features.title: Labor cost forecasting
features.description: Projects wage spend against sales in real time to help managers avoid overtime and stay within budget.

features.title: HR and compliance tools
features.description: Stores employee documents and helps track break, scheduling and labor-law compliance by state and city.

pros: Genuinely usable free Basic plan for very small teams, not just a limited trial
Deep point-of-sale integrations tie labor costs directly to sales data
Combines scheduling, messaging, payroll and hiring in one product
Mobile apps are built specifically for deskless, hourly employees
14-day free trial of the top plan requires no credit card
cons: Unlimited employees only unlock on paid Essentials tier and above
Payroll, tip management and background checks cost extra as add-ons
Advanced HR and compliance tools are locked behind the priciest All-in-One plan
Pricing can add up quickly for multi-location businesses with add-ons enabled
Best suited to hourly/shift businesses, less useful for salaried knowledge-worker teams

faqs.question: Is Homebase free to use?
faqs.answer: Yes. The Basic plan is free forever for up to 10 employees at a single location and includes basic scheduling, time tracking and POS integration. Larger or multi-location teams need a paid Essentials, Plus or All-in-One plan.

faqs.question: How much does Homebase cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at 30 dollars per month for Essentials, rising to 70 dollars per month for Plus and 120 dollars per month for All-in-One, with roughly 20 percent off if billed annually. Payroll, tip management and background checks are separate add-ons.

faqs.question: Does Homebase include payroll?
faqs.answer: Payroll is not bundled by default; it is an add-on priced at 39 dollars per month plus 6 dollars per employee paid, and it runs directly off tracked hours and tips.

faqs.question: What industries use Homebase?
faqs.answer: Homebase is built for hourly, shift-based businesses such as restaurants, retail stores, salons, healthcare practices, and home and repair services.

faqs.question: Does Homebase integrate with POS systems?
faqs.answer: Yes, Homebase integrates with Square, Clover, Toast and Lightspeed, syncing sales data into scheduling and labor cost forecasting.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Homebase?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include When I Work, Deputy, 7shifts (restaurant-focused) and Sling, along with scheduling modules built into POS platforms like Toast and Square.

use_cases.title: Restaurant shift scheduling
use_cases.description: Managers build weekly schedules around sales forecasts from their POS system, reducing both understaffing and overtime costs.

use_cases.title: Multi-location retail time tracking
use_cases.description: Retail chains use GPS- and photo-verified time clocks across locations to prevent time theft and simplify payroll.

use_cases.title: Hourly team hiring and onboarding
use_cases.description: Small businesses post jobs, screen applicants and digitally onboard new hourly hires without separate HR software.
use_cases.audience: Small business owners and HR staff

enrichment.short_description: HomeBox is a free, self-hosted, open-source inventory and organization system for cataloguing household items.
enrichment.seo_title: HomeBox (SysAdminsMedia) Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: HomeBox is a free, self-hosted home inventory app written in Go. See how the SysAdminsMedia fork works, its features and self-hosting requirements.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: HomeBox is a free, self-hosted inventory and organization system designed for home users who want to catalogue household belongings, track warranties and document purchases without relying on a spreadsheet or a paid SaaS product. The project was originally created by a developer known by the GitHub handle hay-kot, who built HomeBox as a lightweight, fast, single-binary application written in Go with an embedded SQLite database and a bundled web UI, explicitly optimized for minimal resource usage so it could run comfortably on a Raspberry Pi or small home server. After the original project stalled in 2024, with its maintainer archiving active development, a collective of IT administrators calling themselves SysAdmins Media stepped in and hard-forked the codebase to continue development under the repository sysadminsmedia/homebox on GitHub. The fork preserved backward compatibility with the original database schema, meaning existing hay-kot HomeBox users could migrate to the new continuation without losing their inventory data. The software is licensed under the GNU Affero General Public License version 3 (AGPL-3.0), which requires that any modified, network-accessible version of the code also make its source available, a common choice for community-maintained self-hosted infrastructure tools. Technically, HomeBox ships as a single Go binary and as Docker images, including standard, rootless and hardened container variants, published to the GitHub Container Registry. A typical deployment runs entirely in a Docker container with idle memory usage generally under 50 megabytes, and the entire stack, backend API, SQLite database and frontend web UI, is embedded in one artifact rather than requiring separate services. Core features include organizing items into categories, locations and nested sub-locations with custom fields, tagging, full-text search across the entire inventory, photo and document attachments per item so users can store receipts, manuals and warranty documents alongside the physical object record, tracking of purchase date, purchase price and current value, and maintenance and warranty expiration tracking with reminders. The interface is a responsive web app that works on desktop, tablet and mobile browsers, and it supports multi-user accounts within a household or shared space. Because it is entirely open source and self-hosted, there is no official pricing or subscription; users run it themselves on their own hardware, a home NAS, a Raspberry Pi, a VPS, or a managed container platform, and third-party hosts such as ElfHosted offer paid managed HomeBox instances for people who do not want to self-host, though that hosting is not operated by the HomeBox project itself. The target audience is technically inclined homeowners, renters, preppers, insurance-conscious households, and self-hosting and homelab enthusiasts who already run other self-hosted services (Home Assistant, Immich, Paperless-ngx, and similar tools) and want a lightweight, private alternative to consumer inventory apps. Competing options include commercial or hybrid home inventory apps such as Sortly and Nest Egg, spreadsheet-based DIY tracking, and other self-hosted asset trackers like Snipe-IT, which is more oriented toward IT asset management than general household belongings. HomeBox differentiates itself through its explicit home-user focus, its extremely small resource footprint compared to heavier self-hosted stacks, its single-binary simplicity that avoids the multi-container complexity of many self-hosted apps, and its active community-driven continuation after the original creator stepped away, which has kept the project receiving regular releases, bug fixes and new features rather than going stale.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HomeBox is a self-hosted, open-source application for tracking everything a household owns, from electronics and furniture to documents and warranties, without handing that data to a third-party cloud service. It was created to be simple to deploy, lightweight to run, and fast to use, favoring a single Go binary and an embedded SQLite database over a heavier multi-service architecture.

The project changed hands in 2024 when its original creator, hay-kot, archived the repository after development stalled. A group of IT administrators operating as SysAdmins Media hard-forked the codebase, migrated the database schema forward, and resumed active development, so existing users could keep their data and continue receiving updates under the new sysadminsmedia/homebox repository.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: HomeBox lets users organize belongings into categories, locations and nested sub-locations, apply tags and custom fields, and search across the entire inventory instantly. Each item can carry photos, attached documents such as receipts or manuals, purchase price and date, and warranty or maintenance expiration tracking with reminders.

Because the whole stack, backend, database and frontend, ships in a single container or binary, deployment is a one-line Docker run command, and the app stays responsive even on very modest hardware like a Raspberry Pi, with idle memory usage generally under 50 megabytes.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: HomeBox is free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license, with no official paid tier, subscription, or usage limits imposed by the project itself. Anyone can download, self-host and modify it at no cost, provided any publicly hosted modified version also shares its source per the AGPL terms.

Users who do not want to manage their own server can pay third-party managed hosting providers, such as ElfHosted, to run a HomeBox instance for them; that hosting fee goes to the third-party provider rather than to the HomeBox project, which does not sell a first-party cloud plan.

features.title: Item organization
features.description: Organize belongings into categories, locations and nested sub-locations with custom fields and tags.

features.title: Full-text search
features.description: Quickly find any item across the entire household inventory by name, tag, location or custom field.

features.title: Photo and document attachments
features.description: Attach photos, receipts, manuals and warranty documents directly to each inventory item.

features.title: Warranty and maintenance tracking
features.description: Track purchase date, price and warranty expiration with reminders for upcoming maintenance.

features.title: Single-binary Go application
features.description: Runs as one lightweight Go binary or Docker container with an embedded SQLite database and web UI.

features.title: Low resource footprint
features.description: Idle memory usage generally stays under 50MB, making it practical to run on a Raspberry Pi or small home server.

features.title: Multi-user households
features.description: Supports multiple user accounts so a household or shared space can jointly manage the same inventory.

features.title: Data migration from the original project
features.description: Preserves the original hay-kot HomeBox database schema so legacy users can upgrade without losing inventory data.

pros: Completely free and open source with no subscription or usage caps
Very lightweight, single-binary architecture that runs on minimal hardware
Self-hosted, keeping household inventory data fully private
Actively maintained again after the SysAdminsMedia fork revived development
Simple Docker deployment with standard, rootless and hardened image variants
cons: Requires self-hosting knowledge (Docker, reverse proxy, backups)
No official first-party managed cloud hosting from the project itself
AGPL-3.0 license imposes source-sharing obligations on modified public deployments
Smaller community and slower feature cadence compared to commercial inventory apps
Mobile experience is a responsive web app rather than a dedicated native app

faqs.question: Is HomeBox free?
faqs.answer: Yes, HomeBox is completely free and open source under the AGPL-3.0 license. There is no official paid tier from the project itself.

faqs.question: Do I need technical skills to run HomeBox?
faqs.answer: Yes, HomeBox is self-hosted, so you need basic familiarity with Docker or running a Go binary on a server, NAS or Raspberry Pi. Non-technical users can pay a third-party host like ElfHosted to run it for them.

faqs.question: What happened to the original HomeBox project?
faqs.answer: The original project by developer hay-kot stalled in 2024 and was archived. A group called SysAdmins Media hard-forked the code and continues active development at sysadminsmedia/homebox on GitHub.

faqs.question: Can I migrate from the original hay-kot HomeBox?
faqs.answer: Yes, the SysAdminsMedia fork migrated the original database schema forward, so existing users can upgrade to the continuation without losing their inventory data.

faqs.question: What tech stack does HomeBox use?
faqs.answer: HomeBox is written in Go with an embedded SQLite database and a bundled web UI, packaged as a single binary or Docker container.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to HomeBox?
faqs.answer: Alternatives include commercial home inventory apps like Sortly, spreadsheet-based tracking, and other self-hosted asset trackers such as Snipe-IT, which is more IT-asset focused.

use_cases.title: Household belongings catalogue
use_cases.description: Homeowners photograph and log furniture, electronics and valuables for insurance and personal reference.

use_cases.title: Warranty and receipt tracking
use_cases.description: Store receipts and warranty documents alongside each item and get reminders before coverage expires.

use_cases.title: Homelab and self-hosting stack
use_cases.description: Self-hosting enthusiasts add HomeBox to an existing Docker-based homelab alongside tools like Home Assistant and Paperless-ngx.

features: Full inventory and organization feature set
Docker, rootless and hardened deployment options

enrichment.short_description: HomeButler is a free, open-source single-binary tool for monitoring, backing up and self-healing a homelab from chat, AI or terminal.
enrichment.seo_title: HomeButler Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: HomeButler is a free, open-source Go binary that monitors, backs up and self-heals a homelab via CLI, web dashboard, or AI tools through MCP.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: HomeButler is an open-source homelab management tool distributed as a single, dependency-free Go binary that gives self-hosters a way to monitor, control, back up and automatically recover their home server infrastructure from the command line, a terminal dashboard, a web UI, or directly through AI assistants like Claude Desktop, ChatGPT and Cursor. It was created by a developer using the GitHub handle Higangssh, who published the project on GitHub under the MIT license and introduced it publicly through Hacker News Show HN posts and a companion DEV Community article titled 'I don't want to give Claude SSH access to my home server,' which explains the project's core design philosophy: AI tools can inspect and act on a homelab through a structured Model Context Protocol (MCP) server that HomeButler exposes, without ever being granted raw shell or SSH access to the underlying machines. This addresses a common concern among homelab operators who want to use AI copilots for infrastructure troubleshooting but do not want to expose their servers to arbitrary command execution by a language model. Technically, HomeButler is built primarily in Go (roughly 94 percent of the codebase) with a Svelte-based frontend embedded directly into the binary via Go's go:embed feature, so there is no separate Node.js runtime or web server to install; the compiled binary bundles a CLI, a terminal UI dashboard built with the Bubble Tea framework, and a full web dashboard in roughly 15 megabytes. Core functionality includes a 'butler report' feature that generates concise health summaries of the homelab with change detection since the last check, a 'doctor' diagnostic command that flags common problems like disk or memory pressure, stopped containers, unexpectedly exposed public ports and stale backups, one-command installation of self-hosted applications via Docker Compose, inventory and topology mapping of running containers and services across multiple servers, crash and restart-loop detection that captures pre- and post-crash logs and flags containers that are repeatedly flapping, Docker volume backup and restore with verification drills to confirm backups are actually restorable, multi-server management over SSH, Wake-on-LAN support for powering on sleeping machines remotely, and the built-in MCP server that lets AI tools query and act on the homelab only through defined, structured JSON operations rather than shell access. Installation is available through Homebrew, a one-line curl installer that auto-detects OS and architecture, npm for the MCP component, or building from source with Go and a Makefile, with GoReleaser used to produce cross-platform release binaries; as of mid-2026 the project had reached roughly its 36th release at version 0.19.2. HomeButler is entirely free and open source with no paid tier, subscription or company behind it in the traditional sense; it functions as an independent, community-driven side project rather than a funded startup, and the GitHub repository shows a modest but active community of around 166 stars and 11 forks at the time of research. Its target audience is homelab operators and self-hosting enthusiasts who already run Docker-based services at home (Plex, Home Assistant, Immich, and similar stacks) and want a lightweight operations layer for monitoring and recovery without deploying a heavier observability stack. Comparable tools include Uptime Kuma for status monitoring, Portainer and Dockge for container management, Netdata for system metrics, and general infrastructure-as-code or backup tools like Duplicati or Restic used piecemeal; HomeButler differentiates by combining monitoring, diagnostics, one-command app installs, backup verification and AI-safe MCP integration into one dependency-free binary rather than requiring several separate self-hosted tools stitched together.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: HomeButler is a free, open-source operations tool built for people who run a homelab, a collection of self-hosted services on a home server or small cluster, and want an easy way to monitor health, catch problems early and recover automatically when something breaks. It ships as a single Go binary with zero external dependencies, so there is nothing else to install to get a CLI, a terminal dashboard, and a web UI running.

The project was created by a developer publishing under the handle Higangssh and was introduced through Hacker News and a DEV Community writeup explaining its central design idea: let AI assistants like Claude, ChatGPT or Cursor help manage a homelab through a structured MCP interface, without ever giving the AI raw SSH or shell access to the machine.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: HomeButler's 'doctor' command diagnoses common homelab issues such as disk and memory pressure, stopped containers, unexpectedly exposed public ports and stale backups, while its 'butler report' produces a concise health summary with change detection over time. It detects container crashes and restart loops, capturing logs before and after failures to help pinpoint root causes.

Beyond diagnostics, it can install self-hosted apps with one command via Docker Compose, map the topology of running containers and services across multiple servers over SSH, back up and restore Docker volumes with verification drills, and wake sleeping machines remotely via Wake-on-LAN. A built-in MCP server exposes all of this to AI tools through structured JSON rather than shell access.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: HomeButler is entirely free and released under the MIT license, with the full source code available on GitHub. There is no paid tier, subscription, or commercial edition; anyone can download the binary or build it from source at no cost.

Because it is a community side project rather than a funded company, there is no enterprise support contract or SLA on offer; users rely on the GitHub issue tracker and community contributions for support, which is typical for a lightweight open-source infrastructure tool of this size.

features.title: Doctor diagnostics
features.description: Scans for disk/memory pressure, stopped containers, exposed public ports and stale backups in one command.

features.title: Butler health reports
features.description: Generates concise homelab health summaries with change detection since the previous check.

features.title: Crash and restart-loop detection
features.description: Captures pre- and post-crash logs and flags containers that are repeatedly flapping.

features.title: One-command app installs
features.description: Deploys self-hosted applications via Docker Compose with a single command.

features.title: Backup and restore with verification
features.description: Backs up Docker volumes and runs verification drills to confirm backups are actually restorable.

features.title: Multi-server management
features.description: Monitors and controls multiple servers remotely over SSH from one binary.

features.title: AI-safe MCP server
features.description: Lets AI tools like Claude Desktop, ChatGPT and Cursor query and act on the homelab through structured JSON, never raw shell access.

features.title: Wake-on-LAN
features.description: Remotely powers on sleeping machines on the local network.

pros: Completely free, MIT-licensed, and fully open source
Single dependency-free Go binary, easy to install and run anywhere
AI integration via MCP is designed to avoid exposing raw shell access
Combines monitoring, diagnostics, backups and app installs in one tool
Very small footprint, suitable for low-power home servers
cons: Community side project with no formal company or SLA-backed support
Smaller GitHub community (roughly 166 stars) compared to established homelab tools
Documentation and feature set still maturing as an actively evolving project
No native mobile app; access is via CLI, TUI or web dashboard
Best suited to technically confident self-hosters, not turnkey for beginners

faqs.question: Is HomeButler free?
faqs.answer: Yes, HomeButler is completely free and open source under the MIT license, with no paid tier or subscription.

faqs.question: What is HomeButler used for?
faqs.answer: It monitors, diagnoses, backs up and self-heals a homelab, letting you manage Docker containers, servers and services from a CLI, terminal dashboard, web UI, or through AI tools via its MCP server.

faqs.question: Does HomeButler give AI tools SSH access to my server?
faqs.answer: No. HomeButler's MCP server exposes homelab data and actions to AI assistants only through structured JSON operations, deliberately avoiding raw shell or SSH access.

faqs.question: What platforms does HomeButler support?
faqs.answer: It ships as a single Go binary installable via Homebrew, a curl script, npm (for the MCP component), or building from source with Go, and manages Docker, systemd and PM2 backed services.

faqs.question: Who created HomeButler?
faqs.answer: It was created by a developer publishing under the GitHub handle Higangssh and introduced publicly via Hacker News and a DEV Community article.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to HomeButler?
faqs.answer: Alternatives include Uptime Kuma for status monitoring, Portainer or Dockge for container management, and Netdata for system metrics, though HomeButler combines several of these functions plus AI-safe integration in one binary.

use_cases.title: Homelab health monitoring
use_cases.description: Self-hosters run the doctor command and butler reports to catch disk, memory or container issues before they cause downtime.

use_cases.title: AI-assisted troubleshooting
use_cases.description: Users connect HomeButler's MCP server to Claude Desktop or Cursor so an AI assistant can help diagnose issues without shell access.
use_cases.audience: Developers using AI coding and ops assistants

use_cases.title: Automated backup verification
use_cases.description: Admins schedule Docker volume backups with restore drills to confirm disaster recovery actually works.
use_cases.audience: Self-hosting enthusiasts managing critical home services

features: Full CLI, TUI and web dashboard
MCP server for AI tool integration

enrichment.short_description: Honeybadger is a bootstrapped error tracking, uptime and check-in monitoring platform built for Ruby, Elixir, PHP, Python and more.
enrichment.seo_title: Honeybadger Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Honeybadger is a bootstrapped error and uptime monitoring tool for Rails, Elixir, PHP and Python. Compare its free, Team and Business pricing plans.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Honeybadger is an application monitoring platform combining exception (error) tracking, uptime monitoring and scheduled job check-ins into a single product aimed primarily at developers working in Ruby on Rails, Elixir, PHP, Python, JavaScript and several other languages and frameworks. The company was founded in 2012 by Ben Curtis, Josh Wood and Starr Horne, three developers who had been using Airbrake, then known as Hoptoad, for error tracking and grew frustrated after Airbrake was acquired in 2011 and its new owners raised prices while service quality declined. The breaking point came when Curtis and Horne received an error notification from their Rails application but found no useful detail in Airbrake's interface; around the same time Wood was independently frustrated by the platform's sluggish UI. The trio spent the summer of 2012 building their own alternative and launched Honeybadger with paying customers in October 2012. The company is headquartered in Kirkland, Washington, and has deliberately remained self-funded and bootstrapped since founding, taking no venture capital; the founders describe answering 'to no one but you, our customer' and prioritize long-term sustainability and profitability over rapid VC-style growth. As of 2026 Honeybadger operates as a small, fully distributed team of roughly 30 people spread across North America, Africa and Asia. Technically, Honeybadger provides SDKs and integrations for Ruby on Rails, Elixir/Phoenix, PHP, Python (Django and Flask), JavaScript/Node.js, Go, Java and other stacks, capturing unhandled exceptions with full stack traces, request context, breadcrumbs and deploy tracking, and correlating errors to specific code deploys and releases. The platform's Uptime Monitoring feature pings URLs or services on a schedule and alerts on downtime, while Check-Ins (sometimes marketed as a heartbeat/cron monitor) verify that scheduled background jobs and cron tasks actually ran, alerting if an expected check-in is missed. A newer Insights feature adds structured application log ingestion and search alongside the error and uptime data, giving a combined operational view. Alerting integrates with Slack, PagerDuty, Microsoft Teams, email and webhooks, and higher tiers add features like SSO, anomaly detection, deployment tracking and status pages that can be shared publicly with customers. Pricing follows a freemium model with three published tiers: the Developer plan is free forever and includes 5,000 errors per month, 50 megabytes per day of log ingestion, 1 monitor, 1 status page, 1 user and 15-day error retention; the Team plan costs 26 dollars per month (or 286 dollars billed annually) and raises limits to 50,000 errors per month, 100 megabytes per day of logging, 5 monitors, unlimited users, 90-day retention and 1 team; and the Business plan costs 80 dollars per month (or 880 dollars annually) with the same base quotas as Team but adds 180-day retention, unlimited teams, SSO and anomaly detection, plus SOC 2 and HIPAA-relevant compliance features for regulated customers. Overage pricing applies beyond the included quota, roughly 0.0003 dollars per notification on Team and 0.0006 dollars per notification on Business, and larger custom enterprise plans are available on request. Honeybadger's target audience is individual developers, startups and small-to-mid-size engineering teams, particularly those on Ruby on Rails and Elixir, who want a focused, developer-friendly monitoring tool without the complexity or enterprise sales process of larger observability vendors. Its most direct competitors are Sentry, Rollbar, Bugsnag and Airbrake itself for error tracking, along with Better Uptime, Pingdom and UptimeRobot for uptime monitoring, and Honeycomb or Datadog for teams that want a fuller observability platform. Honeybadger differentiates through its bootstrapped, developer-first ethos, an all-in-one bundle of error tracking, uptime and check-in monitoring at a lower price than many competitors, and particularly strong first-class support for Ruby on Rails and Elixir communities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Honeybadger began in 2012 when developers Ben Curtis, Josh Wood and Starr Horne grew fed up with their existing error-tracking tool, Airbrake, after it was acquired and service quality declined while prices rose. They built their own alternative and launched with paying customers that same year, choosing to stay self-funded and bootstrapped ever since rather than take venture capital.

The company remains small and distributed, based out of Kirkland, Washington, with a team of roughly 30 people. Its product bundles error tracking, uptime monitoring and scheduled job check-ins into one dashboard, aimed squarely at developers who want fast, no-nonsense visibility into production issues without a heavyweight enterprise observability platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Exception monitoring captures unhandled errors with full stack traces, request context and breadcrumbs, and ties them back to specific deploys so teams can see exactly which release introduced a regression. SDKs cover Ruby on Rails, Elixir/Phoenix, PHP, Python, JavaScript/Node.js, Go and Java, among others.

Uptime Monitoring checks URLs and services on a schedule and alerts on downtime, while Check-Ins verify that scheduled background jobs and cron tasks actually fired, catching silent failures that error tracking alone would miss. Alerts route through Slack, PagerDuty, Microsoft Teams, email and webhooks, and an Insights feature adds structured log search on top.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Honeybadger's Developer plan is free forever and covers 5,000 errors per month, 1 monitor and 1 status page for a single user, making it usable for side projects and very small apps. Paid Team and Business plans raise the quota to 50,000 errors per month and unlock unlimited users.

Team costs 26 dollars per month, or 286 dollars billed annually, while Business costs 80 dollars per month, or 880 dollars annually, and adds longer data retention, unlimited teams, SSO and anomaly detection for compliance-conscious organizations. Usage beyond plan quotas is billed per notification, and custom enterprise pricing is available for larger volumes.

features.title: Exception monitoring
features.description: Captures unhandled errors with full stack traces, request context, breadcrumbs and deploy correlation.

features.title: Uptime monitoring
features.description: Pings URLs and services on a schedule and alerts the team when something goes down.

features.title: Check-Ins (cron monitoring)
features.description: Confirms scheduled background jobs and cron tasks actually ran, alerting on missed check-ins.

features.title: Insights log management
features.description: Ingests and searches structured application logs alongside error and uptime data.

features.title: Deploy tracking
features.description: Correlates errors to specific code deploys so teams can pinpoint which release caused a regression.

features.title: Broad language support
features.description: Official SDKs for Ruby on Rails, Elixir/Phoenix, PHP, Python, JavaScript/Node.js, Go and Java.

features.title: Status pages
features.description: Public status pages that can be shared with customers to communicate incidents and uptime history.

features.title: Alert routing
features.description: Sends alerts to Slack, PagerDuty, Microsoft Teams, email and custom webhooks.

pros: Free Developer plan is usable for real small projects, not just a trial
Bundles error tracking, uptime and cron monitoring in one affordable product
Bootstrapped company with a strong developer-first, no-nonsense reputation
Particularly strong, mature support for Ruby on Rails and Elixir
Simpler pricing and setup than larger enterprise observability platforms
cons: Smaller feature surface than full observability platforms like Datadog or Honeycomb
Free and Team tiers cap error volume at levels that fast-growing apps can outgrow quickly
Longer data retention and SSO require the top Business tier
Smaller company size (about 30 people) means a narrower support and integration ecosystem
Less focused on full distributed tracing compared to dedicated observability tools

faqs.question: Is Honeybadger free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Developer plan is free forever and includes 5,000 errors per month, 1 monitor, 1 status page and 15-day retention for a single user.

faqs.question: How much does Honeybadger cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at 26 dollars per month for Team (50,000 errors/month, unlimited users) and 80 dollars per month for Business, which adds SSO, anomaly detection and longer retention. Annual billing is discounted.

faqs.question: What languages does Honeybadger support?
faqs.answer: Honeybadger provides SDKs for Ruby on Rails, Elixir/Phoenix, PHP, Python, JavaScript/Node.js, Go, Java and several other languages and frameworks.

faqs.question: Is Honeybadger venture-backed?
faqs.answer: No, Honeybadger has been self-funded and bootstrapped since it was founded in 2012, with no outside venture capital investment.

faqs.question: Does Honeybadger monitor uptime and cron jobs, not just errors?
faqs.answer: Yes, in addition to exception monitoring, Honeybadger includes Uptime Monitoring for checking service availability and Check-Ins for verifying scheduled jobs and cron tasks actually ran.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Honeybadger?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Sentry, Rollbar, Bugsnag and Airbrake for error tracking, plus Better Uptime, Pingdom or UptimeRobot for uptime checks, and Honeycomb or Datadog for teams needing broader observability.

use_cases.title: Production error tracking for Rails apps
use_cases.description: Development teams catch and triage unhandled exceptions in Ruby on Rails and Elixir applications, tied to specific deploys.
use_cases.audience: Ruby on Rails and Elixir development teams

use_cases.title: Cron job and background task monitoring
use_cases.description: Teams use Check-Ins to get alerted the moment a scheduled backup, data sync or cron job silently fails to run.

use_cases.title: Startup application monitoring on a budget
use_cases.description: Early-stage startups use the free Developer plan or low-cost Team plan to get error and uptime visibility without enterprise pricing.

enrichment.short_description: Honeycomb is an observability platform for distributed tracing, metrics and debugging production software, built by former Parse and Facebook engineers.
enrichment.seo_title: Honeycomb Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Honeycomb is an observability and tracing platform founded by Charity Majors and Christine Yen. Compare its free, Pro and Enterprise pricing plans.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Honeycomb is an observability platform focused on distributed tracing, high-cardinality event analysis and production debugging for modern, cloud-native software systems. It was founded in 2016 in San Francisco by Charity Majors and Christine Yen, who had previously worked together at Parse, the mobile backend-as-a-service company acquired by Facebook, where they experienced firsthand the difficulty of debugging complex distributed systems with traditional metrics and log-based tools. Majors, who serves as CTO, and Yen, who serves as CEO, built Honeycomb around the idea that engineers need to be able to ask arbitrary, exploratory questions about their production systems in real time rather than being limited to pre-aggregated dashboards, a philosophy the company and its founders have championed publicly as part of the broader 'observability' movement in software engineering, including co-authoring the O'Reilly book 'Observability Engineering.' Honeycomb is headquartered in San Francisco, California, and has raised a total of roughly 150 million dollars in venture funding across five rounds: an initial 4 million dollar seed round, an 11.4 million dollar Series A in 2019, a 20 million dollar Series B in 2020, a 50 million dollar Series C in 2021, and a 50 million dollar Series D in April 2023, backed by investors including Scale Venture Partners and Insight Partners. As of 2026 the company employs roughly 100 to 250 people. Technically, Honeycomb ingests high-cardinality, high-dimensionality event data (wide events) rather than pre-aggregated metrics, and stores it in a proprietary columnar store built for fast, arbitrary querying at scale; it is a founding member and heavy proponent of OpenTelemetry, the open-source instrumentation standard, and supports ingesting OpenTelemetry traces, metrics and logs directly. Core capabilities include distributed tracing across microservices, BubbleUp, a feature that automatically highlights which dimensions correlate with an anomaly or outlier in a trace or query, Service Level Objective (SLO) tracking, triggers and alerting, a query builder for ad hoc exploration without predefined dashboards, and more recently an AI-assisted 'Canvas' copilot and a Honeycomb MCP server that lets AI coding agents query production telemetry directly. Honeycomb also sells a separate Telemetry Pipeline product for routing, filtering and transforming observability data before it reaches a backend, priced from around 0.10 dollars per gigabyte. Pricing follows a freemium model with three tiers: the Free plan costs nothing and includes up to 20 million events per month and up to 100 million metrics data points per month, along with 2 triggers, distributed tracing, BubbleUp, OpenTelemetry support and the Canvas AI Copilot, making it usable for solo developers and small projects; the Pro plan starts at 150 dollars per month, billed monthly or annually, and raises limits to 750 million events per month and 3.75 billion metrics data points per month while adding 100 triggers, 2 SLOs, single sign-on and dedicated Honeycomb support; and the Enterprise plan is custom-priced, typically anchored around a base allowance of 10 billion events annually, and adds 300-plus triggers, 100-plus SLOs, a service map, a Query Data API, AWS PrivateLink connectivity, an SLO Reporting API and private cloud deployment options. Honeycomb's target audience is engineering teams running microservices and distributed, cloud-native architectures, particularly platform, SRE and backend teams that have outgrown traditional metrics-and-logs monitoring and need to debug novel, unpredictable production issues quickly. Its most direct competitors are Datadog, New Relic, Grafana Labs (including Grafana Tempo and Grafana Cloud), Lightstep (now part of ServiceNow), and Honeybadger for teams with lighter needs; Honeycomb differentiates through its high-cardinality, query-first approach to observability rather than dashboard-first metrics, its deep OpenTelemetry alignment, and its founders' thought leadership in defining the modern observability category.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Honeycomb was founded in 2016 in San Francisco by Charity Majors and Christine Yen, both of whom previously worked together at Parse, the mobile backend company acquired by Facebook. Their experience debugging complex distributed systems at Parse convinced them that traditional metrics dashboards and log searches were not enough to understand modern production software, which led directly to Honeycomb's approach of ingesting rich, high-cardinality event data that can be queried on the fly.

The company has become closely associated with popularizing the term 'observability' in software engineering, with Majors and Yen co-authoring the widely referenced book Observability Engineering. Honeycomb has raised approximately 150 million dollars across five funding rounds, most recently a 50 million dollar Series D in 2023, and employs roughly 100 to 250 people out of its San Francisco headquarters.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Honeycomb's core engine ingests wide, high-cardinality events rather than pre-aggregated metrics, enabling engineers to ask arbitrary exploratory questions about production behavior instead of being limited to dashboards defined in advance. Distributed tracing visualizes requests as they flow across microservices, and BubbleUp automatically surfaces which attributes correlate with an anomaly, cutting debugging time significantly.

The platform is a founding proponent of OpenTelemetry and ingests OTel traces, metrics and logs natively. Additional capabilities include Service Level Objectives (SLOs), triggers and alerting, a Service Map for visualizing dependencies, and newer AI features including a Canvas AI Copilot and an MCP server that lets AI coding agents query live telemetry directly.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Honeycomb's Free plan covers up to 20 million events and 100 million metrics data points per month at no cost, which is generous enough for individual projects, testing and small production workloads, and includes tracing, BubbleUp and OpenTelemetry support.

The Pro plan starts at 150 dollars per month for up to 750 million events monthly and adds SSO and more triggers and SLOs, while Enterprise is custom-priced around a 10-billion-events-per-year base allowance and adds a Query Data API, AWS PrivateLink, an SLO Reporting API and private cloud support for large, compliance-sensitive organizations. A separate Telemetry Pipeline product is billed from about 0.10 dollars per gigabyte for data routing and filtering.

features.title: High-cardinality event querying
features.description: Ingests wide, high-cardinality events instead of pre-aggregated metrics, enabling arbitrary ad hoc queries.

features.title: Distributed tracing
features.description: Visualizes requests as they move across microservices to pinpoint latency and failure sources.

features.title: BubbleUp anomaly detection
features.description: Automatically highlights which dimensions correlate with an outlier or anomaly in a query or trace.

features.title: Native OpenTelemetry support
features.description: Ingests OpenTelemetry traces, metrics and logs directly as a founding proponent of the OTel standard.

features.title: SLOs and triggers
features.description: Tracks Service Level Objectives and fires alerts through triggers when thresholds are breached.

features.title: Canvas AI Copilot
features.description: AI-assisted investigation tool that helps engineers explore telemetry data and surface likely causes.

features.title: Honeycomb MCP server
features.description: Lets AI coding agents query live production telemetry directly through the Model Context Protocol.

features.title: Telemetry Pipeline
features.description: A separate product for routing, filtering and transforming observability data before it reaches a backend.

pros: Generous free tier covering up to 20 million events per month
Deep, native OpenTelemetry alignment as a founding proponent of the standard
BubbleUp anomaly detection speeds up root-cause analysis significantly
Query-first architecture supports exploratory questions dashboards cannot answer
Strong thought leadership and documentation from founders active in the observability community
cons: Pro plan starting at 150 dollars per month can be pricier than simpler monitoring tools
Enterprise pricing is custom and not transparent upfront
Learning curve for teams used to traditional dashboard-based monitoring
Fewer built-in integrations for non-technical business metrics compared to broader APM suites
Full observability value requires investing in proper OpenTelemetry instrumentation

faqs.question: Is Honeycomb free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Honeycomb offers a Free plan covering up to 20 million events and 100 million metrics data points per month, forever, with no credit card required.

faqs.question: Who founded Honeycomb?
faqs.answer: Honeycomb was founded in 2016 in San Francisco by Charity Majors (CTO) and Christine Yen (CEO), both former engineers at Parse.

faqs.question: How much does Honeycomb Pro cost?
faqs.answer: Pro plans start at 150 dollars per month for up to 750 million events monthly, billed monthly or annually, and add SSO, more triggers and more SLOs.

faqs.question: Does Honeycomb support OpenTelemetry?
faqs.answer: Yes, Honeycomb is a founding proponent of OpenTelemetry and natively ingests OTel traces, metrics and logs.

faqs.question: What is BubbleUp in Honeycomb?
faqs.answer: BubbleUp is a feature that automatically identifies which dimensions or attributes correlate with an anomaly or outlier, speeding up root-cause debugging.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Honeycomb?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Datadog, New Relic, Grafana Cloud/Tempo, and Lightstep (now part of ServiceNow), along with lighter tools like Honeybadger for smaller teams.

use_cases.title: Microservices debugging
use_cases.description: Platform and SRE teams trace requests across dozens of microservices to find the exact source of latency or errors.

use_cases.title: Production incident response
use_cases.description: On-call engineers use BubbleUp and ad hoc queries to quickly isolate the cause of an ongoing production incident.

use_cases.title: SLO-driven reliability management
use_cases.description: Engineering leadership tracks Service Level Objectives and error budgets across services to prioritize reliability work.

enrichment.short_description: Hookdeck is an event gateway that receives, queues, transforms and reliably delivers webhooks and asynchronous events for developers.
enrichment.seo_title: Hookdeck Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hookdeck is a webhook and event gateway built by Alexandre Bouchard and Maurice Kherlakian. Compare its Developer, Team, Growth and Enterprise pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Hookdeck is an event gateway, essentially managed infrastructure for receiving, queueing, transforming and reliably delivering webhooks and other asynchronous events between services, built for engineering teams that rely on event-driven architectures. It was founded in 2020 by Alexandre Bouchard, who serves as CEO and previously worked as a product designer and full-stack engineer, and Maurice Kherlakian, who serves as CTO and brought more than 20 years of systems architecture experience. The founders built Hookdeck after repeatedly losing hours to missed or mishandled webhook events from providers like Shopify, Stripe and Intercom in their own past projects, and concluded that engineering teams needed a dedicated, purpose-built event gateway rather than hand-rolled webhook receivers and retry logic. The company is headquartered in Montreal, Quebec, Canada, and has raised approximately 5.5 million dollars in funding, including a 2.4 million dollar seed round announced in April 2022 backed by investors such as Matrix Partners, with advisors drawn from companies including Shopify, Checkout.com, Gorgias, LogDNA and Doppler. As a small, focused team of roughly 8 to 10 employees, Hookdeck has positioned itself as core infrastructure that reportedly processes billions of events for thousands of developers and companies. Technically, Hookdeck sits between event sources (Stripe, Shopify, Twilio, GitHub, and any custom webhook sender) and an application's own endpoints, ingesting incoming webhook requests into a durable queue so that a slow or temporarily unavailable receiving service does not cause lost events; it provides automatic retries with configurable backoff, request transformation and filtering rules, fan-out delivery to multiple destinations, and detailed request/response logging so developers can inspect exactly what was sent and received for debugging. It also offers outbound event delivery tooling for companies that need to send reliable webhooks to their own customers, effectively letting a company build a Stripe-style reliable webhook product without building that infrastructure themselves. Hookdeck integrates with local development workflows via a CLI that forwards webhooks to localhost, similar in spirit to tools like ngrok but purpose-built for webhook testing, and supports SOC 2 compliance, SSO/SAML/SCIM for enterprise access control, and metrics export to tools like Datadog on higher tiers. Pricing follows a freemium, usage-based model with four tiers: the Developer plan is always free and includes 10,000 events per month, 1 user and 3-day data retention; the Team plan starts at 39 dollars per month, covers unlimited users, includes 10,000 events with metered overages, and provides 7-day retention plus integrations and community support; the Growth plan starts at 499 dollars per month, adds uptime and latency SLAs, metrics export, SSO/SAML/SCIM and a read-only user role with 30-day retention; and the Enterprise plan is custom-priced with dedicated premium support, a support manager, Slack Connect access and custom security and SLA terms. Beyond the included quota, additional events are billed on a sliding per-100,000-events scale that decreases at higher volumes, from roughly 3.00 dollars per 100,000 events at lower volumes down to about 0.35 dollars per 100,000 events beyond 500 million monthly events, and throughput-based pricing is also available starting around 3.00 dollars per event-per-second at low throughput, declining to about 1.00 dollar per event-per-second at 1,000-plus events per second. Hookdeck's target audience is backend and platform engineering teams building on event-driven or webhook-heavy architectures, including companies integrating deeply with providers like Stripe, Shopify and Twilio, as well as SaaS companies that need to offer reliable outbound webhooks to their own customers. Its most direct competitors are Svix, another dedicated webhooks-as-a-service platform, along with more general message queue and event infrastructure like AWS EventBridge, and lighter-weight local development tools such as ngrok and Webhook.site that only solve part of the problem. Hookdeck differentiates through its focus on reliability (durable queueing, automatic retries, full observability into every event) and by covering both inbound webhook receiving and outbound webhook sending in a single gateway product.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hookdeck was founded in 2020 by Alexandre Bouchard and Maurice Kherlakian in Montreal, Canada, after the pair grew tired of losing critical webhook events from providers like Stripe, Shopify and Intercom due to timeouts, downtime or bugs in their own receiving endpoints. Rather than continuing to hand-roll retry logic and logging for every integration, they built Hookdeck as dedicated infrastructure that sits in front of any application and guarantees webhooks are queued, retried and delivered reliably.

The company has raised roughly 5.5 million dollars, including a 2.4 million dollar seed round backed by Matrix Partners in 2022, and operates as a lean team of under 10 people out of Montreal. It positions itself as an 'event gateway' rather than a narrow webhook tool, covering both receiving inbound events and sending reliable outbound webhooks to customers.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Incoming webhooks are ingested into a durable queue with automatic retries and configurable backoff, so a slow or temporarily down destination does not result in lost events. Developers get detailed request and response logs for every event, transformation and filtering rules to reshape payloads before delivery, and fan-out delivery to route one event to multiple destinations.

For local development, a CLI forwards live webhooks to localhost for easier debugging, and for teams that need to send their own reliable webhooks to customers, Hookdeck offers outbound delivery infrastructure comparable to what large platforms like Stripe build in-house. Enterprise features include SOC 2 compliance, SSO/SAML/SCIM and metrics export to tools like Datadog.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Hookdeck's Developer plan is always free and includes 10,000 events per month with 3-day retention, enough for testing and small projects. The Team plan starts at 39 dollars per month with unlimited users and metered overages beyond the included events.

The Growth plan starts at 499 dollars per month and adds uptime and latency SLAs, SSO/SAML/SCIM and 30-day retention, while Enterprise is fully custom-priced with dedicated support and negotiated SLAs. Additional event volume beyond each plan's quota is billed on a declining per-100,000-events scale, so cost per event drops significantly at higher volumes.

features.title: Durable event queueing
features.description: Ingests inbound webhooks into a queue so temporary downtime or slowness at the destination does not lose events.

features.title: Automatic retries
features.description: Retries failed deliveries with configurable backoff until the destination successfully acknowledges the event.

features.title: Request and response logging
features.description: Records full detail of every event sent and received for debugging and auditing.

features.title: Transformation and filtering
features.description: Reshapes and filters payloads before delivery using configurable rules.

features.title: Fan-out delivery
features.description: Routes a single incoming event to multiple destinations simultaneously.

features.title: Local development CLI
features.description: Forwards live webhooks to localhost for easier debugging during development.

features.title: Outbound webhook infrastructure
features.description: Lets companies send reliable webhooks to their own customers without building that infrastructure themselves.

features.title: Enterprise access controls
features.description: SSO, SAML and SCIM support alongside SOC 2 compliance for regulated organizations.

pros: Always-free Developer tier is genuinely usable for testing and small projects
Reliable queueing and automatic retries prevent lost webhook events
Covers both inbound receiving and outbound sending of webhooks
CLI makes local webhook development and debugging much easier
Founders bring deep, direct experience with the exact problem the product solves
cons: Growth plan at 499 dollars per month is a steep jump from the 39 dollar Team tier
Enterprise pricing is custom and not published
Small team size (under 10 people) compared to larger infrastructure vendors
Data retention on lower tiers (3 to 7 days) is short for deep historical debugging
Usage-based overage pricing requires careful monitoring for high-volume senders

faqs.question: Is Hookdeck free?
faqs.answer: Yes, the Developer plan is always free and includes 10,000 events per month, 1 user and 3-day retention.

faqs.question: Who founded Hookdeck?
faqs.answer: Hookdeck was founded in 2020 by Alexandre Bouchard (CEO) and Maurice Kherlakian (CTO), based in Montreal, Canada.

faqs.question: How much does Hookdeck cost?
faqs.answer: Paid plans start at 39 dollars per month for Team, 499 dollars per month for Growth, and custom pricing for Enterprise, with metered overages for events beyond each plan's included quota.

faqs.question: What is an event gateway?
faqs.answer: An event gateway like Hookdeck sits between event sources such as Stripe or Shopify and your application, queueing, retrying and logging every webhook so events are not lost even if your endpoint is briefly down.

faqs.question: Does Hookdeck help with local webhook development?
faqs.answer: Yes, Hookdeck provides a CLI that forwards live webhooks to localhost, making it easier to debug integrations during development.

faqs.question: What are alternatives to Hookdeck?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Svix for webhooks-as-a-service, AWS EventBridge for general event infrastructure, and lighter tools like ngrok or Webhook.site for local testing only.

use_cases.title: Reliable Stripe and Shopify webhook handling
use_cases.description: Engineering teams route incoming Stripe or Shopify webhooks through Hookdeck to guarantee delivery even during deploys or downtime.

use_cases.title: Outbound webhook infrastructure for SaaS products
use_cases.description: SaaS companies use Hookdeck's outbound delivery tooling to offer reliable webhooks to their own customers without building it in-house.

use_cases.title: Local webhook development and debugging
use_cases.description: Developers use the Hookdeck CLI to forward live webhook events to localhost for faster iteration and debugging.
use_cases.audience: Individual developers and small engineering teams

enrichment.short_description: Hootsuite is a social media management platform for scheduling posts, monitoring engagement and running paid social across all major networks.
enrichment.seo_title: Hootsuite Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hootsuite is a social media scheduling and management platform founded by Ryan Holmes in 2008. Compare its Standard, Professional and Advanced pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Hootsuite is one of the earliest and best-known social media management platforms, providing a unified dashboard for scheduling posts, monitoring conversations, managing a shared inbox, running paid social campaigns and reporting on performance across networks including Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, Pinterest, YouTube, TikTok and X. The company was founded in December 2008 by Ryan Holmes, together with Dario Meli and David Tedman, growing out of an internal tool Holmes built at his Vancouver digital agency, Invoke Media, to manage multiple Twitter accounts. The first version launched on November 28, 2008 as a Twitter dashboard called BrightKit; in February 2009 Holmes crowdsourced a rename contest offering a 500 dollar prize, and a user named Matt Nathan submitted the winning name, Hootsuite, a play on the platform's owl mascot, Owly, and the French phrase 'tout de suite' meaning 'right away.' Hootsuite is headquartered in Vancouver, British Columbia, Canada, and has grown into a global company with roughly 1,500 to 1,900 employees across 13 office locations including Toronto, London, Paris, Sydney, Bucharest, Milan, Luxembourg and Mexico City. The company raised significant funding over its history, including a 1.9 million dollar round in 2009, a 20 million dollar investment in March 2012 that valued the company at 200 million dollars, a 50 million dollar Series A in May 2012, and a 165 million dollar Series B in August 2013, later followed by a 50 million dollar growth round in 2018; it has never gone public and remains privately held. Ryan Holmes served as CEO for over a decade before stepping back, and after CEO Irina Novoselsky departed in April 2026, Holmes returned as interim CEO. Hootsuite has grown through a series of acquisitions, including Seesmic in 2012, AdEspresso and LiftMetrix in 2017, the customer-care platform Sparkcentral in 2021, and the social listening and analytics company Talkwalker in 2024, which significantly expanded Hootsuite's social listening and brand monitoring capabilities. The platform today reports serving over 16 million users across more than 175 countries, managing over 1 million social media accounts. Core functionality includes a unified content calendar and bulk post scheduling across networks, a shared team inbox (Streams) for monitoring and responding to messages and mentions, social listening and competitor and brand monitoring powered by the Talkwalker acquisition, custom performance reporting and analytics, paid social ad management, content approval workflows for larger teams, and an AI assistant called OwlyWriter (referenced in more recent materials as 'Wisdom') that helps draft captions, generate images and surface content recommendations across every plan. Hootsuite removed its long-standing free plan in March 2023 and now sells exclusively on a paid subscription basis with a trial period (roughly 14 to 30 days depending on the offer) rather than a permanent free tier, requiring a credit card to start. Pricing is organized into four tiers: Standard costs 99 dollars per user per month (billed annually) and covers up to 10 social accounts with unlimited scheduled posts, AI-powered post and image generation, a unified inbox and brand/competitor monitoring; Professional costs 199 dollars per user per month and adds unlimited social accounts, inbox automation and 90-day trend forecasting; Advanced costs 399 dollars per user per month and adds content approval workflows, team message routing and team performance metrics; and Enterprise is custom-priced and adds advanced analytics, full social listening, single sign-on and custom compliance solutions, billed via invoice with credit card, check, wire or ACH options. Hootsuite's target audience spans small businesses up through large marketing and communications teams and agencies that need to manage many social accounts, brands or clients from one place. Its most direct competitors are Sprout Social, Buffer, Later, Agorapulse and Sprinklr at the enterprise end; Hootsuite differentiates through its long incumbency and brand recognition in the category, its very broad network integration coverage, and the addition of enterprise-grade social listening after acquiring Talkwalker, though it competes on price against leaner, often cheaper tools like Buffer for smaller teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Hootsuite is one of the original social media management platforms, founded in December 2008 in Vancouver by Ryan Holmes, Dario Meli and David Tedman. It began as an internal Twitter dashboard called BrightKit at Holmes's digital agency, Invoke Media, before a 2009 crowdsourced naming contest turned it into Hootsuite, named after the platform's owl logo, Owly.

Since then, Hootsuite has grown into a global company headquartered in Vancouver with offices in 13 cities and roughly 1,500 to 1,900 employees. It has expanded through acquisitions including Seesmic, AdEspresso, LiftMetrix, Sparkcentral and, most recently, the social listening company Talkwalker in 2024, which significantly broadened its brand monitoring and analytics capabilities.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Hootsuite's dashboard lets teams schedule and publish content in bulk across Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, Pinterest, YouTube, TikTok and X, plan campaigns on a shared content calendar, and monitor and respond to comments and messages from a unified inbox called Streams. Social listening and competitor monitoring, powered by the Talkwalker acquisition, track brand mentions and industry trends at scale.

Higher tiers add content approval workflows, team message routing for customer care, custom performance reporting, and paid social ad management. An AI assistant helps generate captions and images and surface content recommendations, and enterprise customers get single sign-on and custom compliance tooling.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Hootsuite removed its free plan in March 2023 and now sells exclusively through paid subscriptions, starting at 99 dollars per user per month for the Standard plan, which covers up to 10 social accounts. A trial period of roughly two to four weeks is available but requires a credit card upfront.

Professional costs 199 dollars per user per month and unlocks unlimited social accounts and inbox automation, while Advanced costs 399 dollars per user per month and adds approval workflows and team performance metrics. Enterprise pricing is custom and includes full social listening, SSO and compliance features for large organizations.

features.title: Unified content calendar
features.description: Plans and bulk-schedules posts across Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, Pinterest, YouTube, TikTok and X from one dashboard.

features.title: Unified social inbox (Streams)
features.description: Monitors and responds to comments, mentions and messages across every connected network in one place.

features.title: Social listening and monitoring
features.description: Tracks brand mentions, competitors and industry trends at scale, powered by the Talkwalker acquisition.

features.title: AI content assistant
features.description: Generates post captions, images and content recommendations across every pricing tier.

features.title: Custom performance reporting
features.description: Builds custom analytics reports on engagement, reach and campaign performance.

features.title: Paid social ad management
features.description: Manages and optimizes paid social advertising campaigns alongside organic content.

features.title: Content approval workflows
features.description: Routes drafts through multi-step approval chains before publishing, available on Advanced and Enterprise tiers.

features.title: Trend forecasting
features.description: Forecasts upcoming content and industry trends up to 90 days ahead on Professional and higher plans.

pros: Extremely broad network coverage and long track record in the category
Strong social listening and brand monitoring after the Talkwalker acquisition
AI assistant included on every paid tier for content generation
Robust workflow and team collaboration tools for larger marketing teams
Deep third-party integration ecosystem built over more than a decade
cons: No permanent free plan since March 2023, unlike several competitors
Starting price of 99 dollars per user per month is expensive for solo users and small teams
Jump from Standard to Professional (99 to 199 dollars) roughly doubles the cost for unlimited accounts
Enterprise pricing is not published and requires a sales conversation
Interface can feel dated or complex compared to newer, simpler competitors like Buffer

faqs.question: Does Hootsuite have a free plan?
faqs.answer: No, Hootsuite removed its permanent free plan in March 2023. It now offers a trial period of roughly 14 to 30 days on paid plans, requiring a credit card to start.

faqs.question: How much does Hootsuite cost?
faqs.answer: Hootsuite starts at 99 dollars per user per month for the Standard plan (annual billing), rising to 199 dollars for Professional and 399 dollars for Advanced, with custom Enterprise pricing above that.

faqs.question: Who founded Hootsuite?
faqs.answer: Hootsuite was founded in December 2008 by Ryan Holmes, Dario Meli and David Tedman in Vancouver, Canada, evolving from an internal Twitter dashboard at Holmes's agency, Invoke Media.

faqs.question: What social networks does Hootsuite support?
faqs.answer: Hootsuite supports Facebook, Instagram, LinkedIn, Pinterest, YouTube, TikTok and X, among other integrations.

faqs.question: Does Hootsuite include social listening?
faqs.answer: Yes, following its 2024 acquisition of Talkwalker, Hootsuite includes brand and competitor monitoring, with full social listening available on higher-tier plans.

faqs.question: What are the main alternatives to Hootsuite?
faqs.answer: Common alternatives include Sprout Social, Buffer, Later, Agorapulse and, at the enterprise end, Sprinklr.

use_cases.title: Multi-brand social scheduling
use_cases.description: Marketing teams schedule and publish content across dozens of social accounts and brands from a single calendar.
use_cases.audience: Marketing teams and social media managers

use_cases.title: Brand and competitor monitoring
use_cases.description: Communications teams track brand mentions, sentiment and competitor activity across social platforms in real time.

use_cases.title: Agency client management
use_cases.description: Digital agencies manage social accounts and reporting for multiple clients from one Hootsuite workspace.
use_cases.audience: Digital marketing and social media agencies

enrichment.short_description: Hoppscotch is an open-source API development ecosystem for testing REST, GraphQL, WebSocket and other protocols, built as a Postman alternative.
enrichment.seo_title: Hoppscotch Review 2026: Features, Pricing and Alternatives
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Hoppscotch is an open-source API testing tool and Postman alternative founded by Liyas Thomas. Compare its free, Organization and self-host pricing.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary: Hoppscotch is an open-source API development ecosystem that lets developers build, test, document and share API requests across REST, GraphQL, WebSocket, Server-Sent Events, Socket.IO and MQTT protocols, positioned as a lightweight, fast and fully open-source alternative to commercial tools like Postman and Insomnia. The project was created by Liyas Thomas, a developer based in Kochi, Kerala, India, who first launched it in 2019 under the name Postwoman as a browser-based API request builder. On its first anniversary in 2020 the project was rebranded to Hoppscotch, with the new name crowdsourced from its community, and it has since grown into one of the most starred open-source developer tools on GitHub, surpassing roughly 78,000 to 80,000 stars and counting organizations such as Google, Microsoft, Salesforce and IBM among its users. Hoppscotch operates as a company, Hoppscotch Inc, headquartered in Kochi, India, and raised a 3 million dollar seed round in December 2021 led by OSS Capital, with participation from Automattic, the company behind WordPress, and angel investors including Austen Allred; OSS Capital founder Joseph Jacks joined Hoppscotch's board following the round. The company remains a lean, largely remote team, reported at anywhere from roughly 8 to 20 people depending on the source, reflecting its open-source, community-driven development model where a large share of feature development comes from outside contributors. Technically, the Hoppscotch web and desktop client is built primarily with Vue.js and TypeScript on the frontend, with a Rust component used for performance-sensitive request handling, and the product ships as a browser-based Progressive Web App with offline support, a downloadable desktop application, and a command-line interface for running collections in CI/CD pipelines; the self-hostable backend and admin dashboard are also open source, licensed under the MIT license, and can be deployed via Docker for organizations that want to keep all API testing data on their own infrastructure. Core features include a request builder supporting all major HTTP methods plus GraphQL, WebSocket, SSE, Socket.IO and MQTT, collections and folders for organizing requests, environment variables for managing different deployment targets, pre-request and post-request scripting for automated tests and dynamic values, request history, team workspaces with role-based access control for collaboration, a built-in proxy to work around CORS restrictions when testing browser-inaccessible APIs, and a customizable, themeable interface.
